compliance_mappings.notes: Both establish access control policy governance requirements including purpose, scope, roles, and periodic review
ISO A.5.1 is broader covering all information security policies; NIST AC-1 is specific to access control policy
Both address full identity lifecycle management including creation, modification, and removal of accounts
ISO A.5.18 covers access rights provisioning/review which is a subset of NIST AC-2 account management scope
ISO A.5.9 inventory of assets relates to the account inventory requirements within NIST AC-2
ISO A.5.18 includes automated provisioning concepts but NIST AC-2(1) is more explicit about automated account management mechanisms
ISO A.5.18 addresses timely removal of access but NIST AC-2(2) specifically mandates automatic removal of temporary and emergency accounts
ISO A.5.18 requires regular review of access rights; NIST AC-2(3) specifically requires automatic disabling after a defined inactivity period
Both require audit logging of account lifecycle events including creation, modification, enabling, disabling, and removal
ISO A.8.5 covers secure authentication broadly; NIST AC-2(5) specifically addresses per-session inactivity logout requirements
Both address privileged account management including separation from regular accounts, role-based assignment, and periodic review
ISO A.5.16 covers shared identity scenarios; NIST AC-2(9) adds specific restrictions and supplementary accountability controls for shared accounts
ISO A.5.15 covers access control rules generally; NIST AC-2(11) specifically addresses context-based usage conditions like time, location, and device
Both require monitoring for anomalous behaviour patterns with baseline establishment and deviation alerting
ISO A.5.18 covers prompt removal of access; NIST AC-2(13) specifically addresses rapid disabling for individuals determined to pose significant risk
ISO A.6.5 covers termination responsibilities; NIST AC-2(13) extends to high-risk users under investigation, not just terminated employees
ISO A.5.15 defines access control policy; NIST AC-3 focuses on the technical enforcement of approved authorizations through ACLs, RBAC, ABAC mechanisms
Both require technical enforcement of access restrictions at all access points including applications, APIs, and databases
ISO A.5.3 addresses segregation of duties; NIST AC-3(2) specifically addresses dual authorization (two-person integrity) for critical operations
ISO A.8.3 covers information access restriction broadly; NIST AC-3(4) specifically addresses discretionary access control (DAC) model
ISO A.5.15 mentions access control models; NIST AC-3(7) specifically mandates role-based access control with detailed role management requirements
ISO A.5.18 covers access modification; NIST AC-3(8) specifically addresses real-time revocation when security attributes change
ISO A.5.15 covers access control broadly; NIST AC-3(13) specifically mandates attribute-based access control (ABAC) with subject, object, action, and environment attributes
ISO A.8.3 covers information access restriction; NIST AC-4 addresses information flow control between systems and across boundaries, a distinct concept
ISO A.5.14 covers information transfer rules; NIST AC-4 addresses technical enforcement of information flow policies
ISO A.8.3 covers access restriction; NIST AC-4(1) specifically requires security attribute binding to information objects for data-centric flow control
Both require segregation of conflicting duties to reduce fraud, error, and insider threat risk through role separation
ISO A.5.15 mentions least privilege as part of access control policy; NIST AC-6 is a dedicated control with detailed enforcement requirements
ISO A.8.2 covers privileged access restriction; NIST AC-6 is broader covering least privilege for all users and processes
ISO A.8.2 covers privileged access; NIST AC-6(1) specifically requires explicit authorization for access to security functions and information
Both require separation of privileged and non-privileged accounts, mandating dedicated admin accounts for administrative tasks
ISO A.8.2 covers privileged access generally; NIST AC-6(3) specifically restricts network access to privileged commands through jump servers and PAWs
Both require restricting privileged accounts to a defined set of personnel with regular review and justification documentation
Both require periodic review of user privileges to validate continued need and remove excess permissions (privilege creep prevention)
Both require audit logging of privileged function execution with user identification, function executed, and outcome
ISO A.8.2 covers privileged access restriction; NIST AC-6(10) specifically prevents non-privileged users from executing privileged functions through technical controls
ISO A.8.5 covers secure authentication broadly; NIST AC-7 specifically addresses unsuccessful logon attempt limits, lockout, and delay mechanisms
Both require displaying acceptable use and monitoring notification before granting access to systems
ISO A.8.5 covers secure authentication; NIST AC-10 specifically limits concurrent sessions per account to detect credential sharing and theft
ISO A.8.1 covers endpoint device protection including screen lock; NIST AC-11 specifically addresses session lock after inactivity with re-authentication requirement
ISO A.8.5 covers secure authentication procedures; NIST AC-12 specifically addresses automatic session termination conditions and resource release
ISO A.5.15 defines access control policy; NIST AC-14 specifically identifies and documents permitted actions without authentication
ISO A.8.3 covers information access restriction; NIST AC-16 specifically addresses security and privacy attributes (classification labels, sensitivity tags) for ABAC
ISO A.8.1 covers endpoint device protection; NIST AC-17 specifically addresses remote access usage restrictions, authorization, and encryption requirements
ISO A.8.20 covers network security; NIST AC-17 specifically addresses remote access methods including VPN, SSH, and web portal security
ISO A.8.16 covers general monitoring; NIST AC-17(1) specifically addresses monitoring and control of remote access sessions and anomalous patterns
ISO A.8.24 covers cryptography use; NIST AC-17(2) specifically mandates encryption for remote access session confidentiality and integrity
ISO A.8.2 covers privileged access; NIST AC-17(4) specifically addresses remote execution of privileged commands with session recording
ISO A.8.20 covers network security broadly; NIST AC-18 specifically addresses wireless access restrictions and WPA3 Enterprise authentication
Both address mobile device access control including enrollment, configuration enforcement, containerization, and remote wipe capability
ISO A.5.19 covers supplier relationship security; NIST AC-20 specifically addresses access from external systems including partner networks and personal devices
ISO A.5.20 covers supplier agreement security requirements; NIST AC-20 establishes terms for access from external systems with compensating controls
ISO A.5.15 covers access control policy; NIST AC-21 specifically addresses user-driven information sharing decisions based on partner authorization matching
ISO A.8.3 covers information access restriction; NIST AC-22 specifically addresses publicly accessible content review to prevent nonpublic information disclosure
ISO A.5.15 covers access control rules; NIST AC-24 specifically addresses centralized vs local access decision architecture (PDP/PEP model)
ISO A.8.3 covers access restriction enforcement; NIST AC-25 specifically mandates a tamperproof reference monitor that mediates all access
ISO A.5.15 covers access control policy broadly; NIST IA-1 specifically addresses identification and authentication policy with authenticator types and identity proofing
ISO A.8.5 covers secure authentication procedures; NIST IA-1 establishes the governance framework including acceptable authenticator types per system
Both require secure authentication of organizational users with mechanisms appropriate to system sensitivity
ISO A.5.16 covers identity management; NIST IA-2 covers both unique identification and authentication as a combined control
ISO A.8.5 mentions MFA for critical systems; NIST IA-2(1) specifically mandates MFA for all network access to privileged accounts with phishing-resistant preference
ISO A.8.2 covers privileged access management; NIST IA-2(1) adds the specific MFA requirement for all privileged account authentication
ISO A.8.5 covers secure authentication; NIST IA-2(6) specifically requires one MFA factor from a separate device for single-point-of-compromise prevention
Both address authenticator management including initial distribution, strength requirements, lost/compromised handling, and lifecycle procedures
ISO A.5.17 covers authentication information management generally; NIST IA-5(1) specifically addresses password complexity, breach-checking, and storage requirements
ISO A.8.24 covers cryptography use; NIST IA-5(1) specifically requires approved key derivation functions for password storage
ISO A.5.19 covers supplier relationship security; NIST IA-8 specifically addresses authentication of non-organizational users including contractors and federated users
ISO A.8.5 covers secure authentication; NIST IA-11 specifically addresses re-authentication triggers including time elapsed, sensitive resource access, and context changes
ISO A.5.16 covers identity management; NIST IA-12 specifically addresses identity proofing with graduated assurance levels (IAL1-3) before credential issuance
ISO A.6.1 covers personnel screening; NIST IA-12 extends identity proofing beyond screening to include government-issued ID verification for higher assurance
ISO A.5.16 covers identity verification; NIST IA-12(2) specifically addresses validation and verification of identity evidence including biometric comparison
ISO A.5.15 covers access control policy broadly including logical and physical access; NIST AC-1 is narrower focusing on logical access control policy
Both address full identity lifecycle from creation through removal with unique identification and formal processes
Both address authenticator/credential management including allocation, protection, and lifecycle control
NIST AC-2 is broader covering all account management; ISO A.5.18 focuses specifically on access rights provisioning, review, and removal
NIST AC-6(7) specifically covers periodic review of user privileges; ISO A.5.18 covers broader access rights review including provisioning and removal
Both restrict privileged access to designated personnel with separate accounts, competence verification, and frequent review
Both require technical enforcement of access restrictions through ACLs, RBAC, ABAC at all access points
Both require secure authentication with MFA for critical systems and credential protection during transmission and storage
Both address segregation of conflicting duties through role separation and technical workflow enforcement
ISO A.8.15 covers all logging requirements broadly; NIST AC-2(4) is specifically focused on account lifecycle event auditing
ISO A.8.16 covers all monitoring activities; NIST AC-2(12) specifically addresses atypical account usage monitoring
Both address cryptographic key management including generation, distribution, storage, rotation, and destruction for identity infrastructure
NIST AC-20 covers external system access; ISO A.5.19 is broader covering all supplier relationship security including IAM assessments
NIST IA-12 covers full identity proofing; ISO A.6.1 covers personnel screening which supports but does not fully replace identity proofing
NIST AC-2(13) covers high-risk user account disabling; ISO A.6.5 covers broader termination and change-of-role responsibilities
ISO A.8.1 covers all endpoint devices broadly; NIST AC-19 specifically covers mobile device access control
Both require acceptable use rules and system use notification before granting access
